title: Additional Resources: Coding
text: This page is a guide for choosing the right resource when you get stuck. The list is intentionally curated rather than exhaustive. Start with the resource that matches your problem, then go deeper if you need more detail.

title: Additional Resources: Coding
text:
Start here



If you need help with…
Start with…
Useful for




learning R and tidy data
R for Data Science
Data Basics, Data Integration


dplyr, tidyr, or ggplot2 syntax
Posit Cheatsheets
All workshops

Setup, collaboration


GitHub collaboration

Group final projects


vector and raster concepts

Vector Data, Raster Data


spatial data in R
Introduction to Geospatial Raster and Vector Data with R
Vector Data, Raster Data, Spatial Visualization


writing a report
Quarto Guide
Final project reports


model interpretation
randomForest package manual
Random Forest, Sensitivity Analysis

title: Additional Resources: Coding
section: What to use when you are stuck
text:
What to use when you are stuck



Problem
Resource




“I cannot push to GitHub.”
Happy Git troubleshooting


“I do not understand joins.”
R4DS joins


“My data are wide when they should be long.”
R4DS data tidying


“My plot is not showing what I expect.”
R4DS data visualization and the ggplot2 cheatsheet


“My vector and raster layers do not line up.”
Data Carpentry geospatial concepts and terra spatial data tutorial


“I need to crop, mask, resample, or project rasters.”
terra reference site


“I need to buffer, join, or measure vector geometries.”
sf reference site and sf cheatsheet


“I do not know what a flux variable means.”
AmeriFlux data variables


“I need climate rasters for a final project.”
ERA5 documentation


“I need daily climate for a point location.”
Daymet overview


“I need wetland/inundation area for methane scaling.”
WAD2M paper

title: Additional Resources: Coding
text:
Git and GitHub

Collaborative Coding with GitHub: A beginner-friendly workshop on using Git with GitHub and RStudio. This is especially useful for group work because it emphasizes workflows, conflicts, branches, issues, and project documentation.
Happy Git and GitHub for the useR: A comprehensive guide to Git, GitHub, and RStudio. Use the troubleshooting page when authentication, pushing, pulling, or remotes get weird.
NCEAS Introduction to Git and GitHub and Collaborating with Git and GitHub: Clear lesson-style materials for learning Git commands and collaboration patterns.

title: Additional Resources: Coding
text:
Tidyverse and data wrangling

R for Data Science: The best general-purpose reference for tidy data workflows. Particularly useful sections include data visualization, data transformation, joins, tidying, functions, and Quarto.
Coding in the Tidyverse: A workshop covering dplyr, tidyr, and ggplot2 for wrangling, summarizing, reshaping, joining, and visualizing data.
Posit Cheatsheets: Quick references for common package syntax. Keep the dplyr, tidyr, and ggplot2 cheatsheets close while working through the course.
NCEAS Cleaning and Wrangling Data: A lesson on tidy data and wrangling that pairs well with Data Basics and Data Integration.

title: Additional Resources: Coding
text:
Spatial data

Introduction to Geospatial Concepts: A conceptual introduction to raster data, vector data, coordinate reference systems, spatial resolution, and extent.
Introduction to Geospatial Raster and Vector Data with R: A practical workshop for manipulating and plotting raster and vector data with terra, sf, and ggplot2.
sf reference site: Function documentation and examples for vector data. Use this when working with points, polygons, joins, intersections, buffers, and distances.
Spatial manipulation with sf cheatsheet: A quick reminder sheet for common sf operations.
terra reference site and Spatial Data with terra: Documentation and tutorials for raster workflows such as cropping, masking, projecting, resampling, extracting, stacking, and predicting.
NCEAS Working with Spatial Data: A lesson using sf, dplyr, ggplot2, and leaflet to manipulate and visualize vector data.

title: Additional Resources: Coding
text:
Reporting and reproducibility

Quarto Guide: The main reference for writing reproducible reports, websites, slides, and manuscripts with code and narrative text together.
Quarto Markdown Basics: Use this when formatting headings, lists, links, figures, tables, and code blocks.
Quarto Computations: Using R: Use this when controlling code output, messages, warnings, figures, and execution options in .qmd reports.
R4DS Quarto chapter: A friendly introduction to using Quarto for reproducible data analysis.

title: Additional Resources: Coding
text:
Modeling and interpretation

randomForest package manual: Reference for randomForest(), predict(), importance(), and varImpPlot(). Use this when checking arguments such as ntree, mtry, and importance.
R4DS model basics: A gentle introduction to model thinking in R. It does not replace the course Random Forest workshop, but it is useful for thinking about predictors, response variables, and model diagnostics.
Tidymodels Learn: A broader modeling resource. This course uses randomForest directly, but tidymodels materials are useful for understanding resampling, recipes, model comparison, and evaluation metrics.

title: Additional Resources: Coding
text:
Environmental data products

AmeriFlux data variables: Use this when interpreting flux variable names, timestamps, gap-filled variables, and quality-control conventions.
FLUXNET2015 dataset: Background on the global flux dataset used in many ecosystem modeling studies.
Daymet overview: Background on gridded daily weather data for North America. Useful for climate summaries around Yale-Myers Forest and other point locations.
daymetr package: R package for downloading Daymet data directly into R.
ERA5 data documentation: Reference for ERA5 climate and reanalysis variables used in Data Integration and Spatial Model Visualization.
WAD2M paper: Background on the Wetland Area and Dynamics for Methane Modeling product used to scale spatial methane flux predictions by inundated area.

title: Additional Resources: Coding
text:
How to use resources well
When you get stuck, write down three things before searching:

What object are you working with? For example, data.frame, sf, SpatRaster, model object, or Quarto document.
What operation are you trying to do? For example, join, summarize, crop, project, predict, render, or push.
What error message or unexpected result did you get?

Then search using the package name and function name together, such as terra resample, sf st_join, dplyr left_join, randomForest importance, or Quarto code chunk options.

title: Additional Resources: Data Repositories
text:
This page lists data repositories and products that can support environmental data science projects. For this course, use it as a final-project planning guide: start with the response variable you want to model, then identify climate, vegetation, soil, land-cover, wetland-area, and validation data that match your study region and time period.
This list builds from resources shared by the LTER Network’s Synthesis Skills for Early Career Researchers course and Ecological Data Synthesis: A Primer on Essential Methods course.

title: Additional Resources: Data Repositories
section: Start with your final project need
text:
Start with your final project need



Final project need
Good places to start
Notes




methane, carbon, water, or energy fluxes
AmeriFlux, FLUXNET, NEON
Check variable names, timestamps, gap filling, units, and site metadata.


daily point climate
Daymet
Useful for U.S. and North American site climate summaries.


global monthly climate or reanalysis
ERA5, NOAA
ERA5 works well for gridded predictor rasters but requires careful unit conversion.


vegetation index or land cover
NASA AppEEARS, Google Earth Engine, Microsoft Planetary Computer
Useful for NDVI, EVI, MODIS land cover, and other remote sensing products.


wetland or inundation area
WAD2M, NASA
Needed when converting methane flux density to emissions.


soils, elevation, hydrology, or disturbance
USGS, EES-DIVE, NEON
Useful for static site predictors and interpretation.


ecological datasets with metadata
EDI, KNB, DataONE
Good sources for reusable ecological datasets and examples of published metadata.


biodiversity or trait context
GBIF, Open Traits Network
Useful when projects include species distributions, plant traits, or ecosystem context.

title: Additional Resources: Data Repositories
text:
Repository table



Name
Best use in this course
R package or access route




AmeriFlux
Tower flux observations, site metadata, and variable conventions for carbon, water, energy, and methane studies.
amerifluxr

Global flux tower data and background for cross-site modeling.
website download

U.S. ecological observations, meteorology, remote sensing, and site metadata.
neonUtilities


Daymet
Daily gridded weather for North America, especially point-based climate summaries.
daymetr


ERA5 Climate Data Store
Global reanalysis predictors such as precipitation, temperature, and vapor pressure variables.
ecmwfr


NASA AppEEARS
Point or area downloads for MODIS, VIIRS, vegetation indices, land cover, and other remote-sensing products.
website download


NASA Open Data
NASA-hosted environmental data products, including examples of methane upscaling products.
website/API


WAD2M
Monthly wetland/inundation fraction for scaling methane flux density to emissions.
data product download


Google Earth Engine
Cloud analysis of satellite imagery and large gridded datasets.
rgee


Microsoft Planetary Computer
Cloud-hosted environmental and remote-sensing datasets.
rstac


EDI
Long-term ecological datasets and examples of well-documented data packages.
EDIutils


KNB
Ecological and environmental data packages with metadata and DOIs.
dataone


DataONE
Search across many environmental data repositories.
dataone


EES-DIVE
Environmental system science datasets, model inputs, and data products.
website/API


GBIF
Species occurrence and biodiversity records.
rgbif


Open Traits Network
Trait dataset inventory and links to trait repositories.
varies


PhenoCam Network
Camera-derived vegetation phenology data.
phenocamapi


NOAA OneStop
Weather, climate, oceanographic, and atmospheric data.
varies


USGS
Hydrology, geology, topography, land cover, and other environmental data.
dataRetrieval


US National Park Service
Park boundaries, vegetation, geology, air quality, and water quality data.
NPSutils

title: Additional Resources: Data Repositories
text:
Before using a dataset
For each dataset you plan to use, record:

The citation or URL.
The variable names and units.
The spatial resolution and coordinate reference system.
The temporal resolution and years available.
Whether the data are raw, gap-filled, modeled, or remotely sensed.
The license or data-use policy.
Any processing or unit conversion needed before modeling.

This table becomes part of your final project methods section.

title: Random Forest Models: Predicting Methane Flux
text:
In Data Integration, we built the kind of table needed for methane flux modeling: one row per site-month, with methane flux as the response and climate, vegetation, and ecosystem variables as candidate predictors.
In this workshop, we will ask:

Can we use climate, vegetation, and ecosystem predictors to estimate monthly methane flux, and how do we know whether the model is useful?

By the end of this workshop, you will be able to:

Identify a response variable and candidate predictors.
Prepare a model-ready table for random forest regression.
Compare candidate random forest models.
Evaluate train and test performance using RMSE, MAE, R-squared, and bias.
Interpret variable importance without treating it as causation.
Compare random row splits with leave-one-site-out assessment.
Build a final model by selecting the best predictor from each category.
Save a final model object for sensitivity analysis and spatial prediction.




install.packages("randomForest")
install.packages("tidyverse")
install.packages("GGally")





library(randomForest)
library(tidyverse)
library(GGally)

title: ENV 730
text: Th 2:30pm-5:20pm in Kroon 319

title: ENV 730
text: Th 2:30pm-5:20pm in Kroon 319

title: ENV 730
text:
Instructor
Dr. Sparkle L. Malone
sparkle.malone@yale.edu
Office Hours: Mondays 12pm-3pm
Teaching Fellow: TBD

title: ENV 730
text:
Limited Enrollment
Prerequisites:

R Series Training

title: ENV 730
text:
Format
The primary format is synchronous, in-person lectures, discussions, and workshops.
Course Description: In today’s world, understanding environmental data and making informed decisions based on it is crucial for addressing complex environmental challenges. This course serves as an introductory exploration into the integration of environmental data using R programming language, coupled with machine learning techniques. Participants will gain hands-on experience in handling, analyzing, and interpreting environmental datasets, with a focus on leveraging the power of R for data integration and predictive modeling.

title: ENV 730
text:
Learning Objectives

We will use coding and reproducible best practices.
We will become familiar with different types of environmental data.
We will use machine learning to explore adaptive management solutions.

title: ENV 730
text:
Required Materials

R for Data Science
R For Earth System
Spatial Data Science with R and “terra”

Computing needs: Please bring a laptop to class. Your laptop must have both R and Rstudio installed and operational prior to the start of this course. Ideally you will have 16GB of RAM or higher, dual Core 2Ghz or higher (Intel Core i5 processor or equivalent) and your operating system should be Windows 10 or higher or OS X 10.14 or higher.

title: ENV 730
text:
Course Policies
Attendance Policy: Regular in-person classroom attendance is required of all students. When extenuating circumstances require accommodation, Dr. Malone will arrange for remote attendance via zoom.
Late Policy: All assignments submitted after the due date are subject to a late penalty. For the first 24 hour period beyond an assignment’s due date there is a 10% penalty, which increases by 10% for each additional 24 hour period.
Academic integrity is a core university value that ensures respect for the academic reputation of the University, its students, faculty and staff, and the degrees it confers. The University expects that students will conduct themselves in an honest and ethical manner and respect the intellectual work of others. Please ask about my expectations regarding permissible or encouraged forms of student collaboration if they are unclear. Any work that you submit at any stage of the writing process—thesis, outline, draft, bibliography, final submission, presentations, blog posts, and more—must be your own; you also may not use material generated by ChatGPT or any other AI writing software. In addition, any words, ideas, or data that you borrow from other people and include in your work must be properly documented. Failure to do either of these things is plagiarism. -Poorvu Center
Before collaborating with an AI chatbot on your work for this course, please request permission by sending me a note that describes (a) how you intend to use the tool and (b) how using it will enhance your learning. Any use of AI to complete an assignment must be acknowledged in a citation that includes the prompt you submitted to the bot, the date of access, and the URL of the program. -Poorvu Center
Diversity and Disability Statement: Our institution values diversity and inclusion; we are committed to a climate of mutual respect and full participation. Our goal is to create learning environments that are usable, equitable, inclusive and welcoming. If there are aspects of the instruction or design of this course that result in barriers to your inclusion or accurate assessment or achievement, please notify Dr. Malone as soon as possible. Disabled students are also welcome to contact Student Accessibility ServicesLinks to an external site. to discuss a range of options to removing barriers in the course, including accommodations. -Poorvu Center
Usability, Disability and Design: I am committed to creating a course that is inclusive in its design. If you encounter barriers, please let me know immediately so that we can determine if there is a design adjustment that can be made or if an accommodation might be needed to overcome the limitations of the design. I will consider creative solutions, as long as they do not compromise the intent of the assessment or learning activity. You are also welcome to contact Student Accessibility ServicesLinks to an external site. to begin this conversation or to establish accommodations for this or other courses. I welcome feedback that will assist me in improving the usability and experience for all students. -Poorvu Center

title: ENV 730
text:
Assignments, Assessments & Grading

Exam (125 points): This exam is designed to evaluate basic knowledge of R and Rstudio. A score of 90% or higher signifies that students have a strong foundational knowledge of R and R studio and are prepared to focus on the content in this course.
Attendance: Regular in-person classroom attendance is required of all students. When extenuating circumstances require accommodation, Dr. Malone will arrange for remote attendance via zoom.
Post-workshop Assessments (~150 points): Assessments are assignments that require direct application of tools and analysis approaches.
Major Assessments (200 points): Activities designed to test mastery of concepts.
Final Project (200 points):

Proposal Presentation (50 points)
Final Report (100 points)
Final Project Presentation (50 points)

title: Final Project Roadmap
text:
The final project asks you to build, evaluate, interpret, and spatialize a methane flux model. Each workshop creates one part of that workflow.

title: Final Project Roadmap
text:
Final project workflow

Define a methane flux question and study region.
Inventory candidate response data and predictors.
Compile a clean modeling table with clear join keys.
Compare model formulas and validation strategies.
Fit a final model and evaluate performance.
Test sensitivity and extrapolation risk.
Project the model in space only where all predictors can be mapped.
Scale flux density by wetland or inundated area.
Interpret results, limitations, and next data needs.

title: Final Project Roadmap
Final Project Roadmap
text:
Standard workshop deliverable
Unless a workshop gives different instructions, use this structure for short submissions:

One clear question.
One reproducibility note describing the data and code used.
One table.
One or two figures.
One interpretation paragraph.
One limitation or next-step paragraph.

title: Final Project Roadmap
text:
Final project rubric







Category
What strong work shows




Data integration
Data sources, units, keys, missingness, and scale mismatches are documented and checked.


Spatial reasoning
Vector and raster operations use appropriate CRS, resolution, extent, and extraction methods.


Model development
Candidate models are compared with a clear response variable, predictors, and validation metrics.


Validation
Random train/test assessment is paired with a site-aware assessment such as leave-one-site-out.


Sensitivity and uncertainty
The report distinguishes model importance, sensitivity, extrapolation, and uncertainty.


Spatial scaling
Flux density is converted to emissions using correct area and unit conversions.


Interpretation
Results are connected to methane ecology, tower representativeness, and study-site limitations.


Reproducibility
Code, data products, package needs, and generated outputs are organized and described.

title: Final Project Roadmap
text:
Glossary







Term
Meaning




flux density
A rate per area, such as g C m-2 month-1.


emissions
Total mass emitted over an area and time period, such as Tg CH4 year-1.


inundation fraction
The fraction of a grid cell that is wetland or inundated area.


WAD2M
Wetland Area and Dynamics for Methane Modeling, a monthly inundation/wetland fraction product.


representativeness
How well available observations cover the conditions where predictions will be made.


dissimilarity
A distance measure describing how different sites are in predictor space.


extrapolation
Prediction for conditions outside the training data.


RMSE
Root mean squared error; lower values indicate smaller average prediction errors.


bias
Mean predicted value minus mean observed value.


leave-one-site-out
A validation strategy that trains on all but one site, then tests on the held-out site.


CRS
Coordinate reference system, the spatial coordinate framework for a map layer.


resolution
Pixel or grid-cell size for raster data.


extent
The spatial bounding box covered by a layer.


static predictor
A predictor that does not change through time in the modeling table.


dynamic predictor
A predictor that changes through time and must be joined by site and date.

title: Final Project Roadmap
text:
Troubleshooting







Symptom
Check first




Package will not load
Install it, restart R, and confirm the package name is spelled correctly.


Quarto render fails
Read the first error, run that chunk interactively, then fix the object or package named in the error.


Join creates too many rows
Check whether the join keys are unique before joining.


Join creates many NA values
Check spelling, date classes, site IDs, and coordinate precision.


Spatial layers do not overlap
Compare CRS, extent, and coordinate units.


Raster stack will not combine
Check CRS, resolution, extent, and number of layers.


terra::predict() returns NA
Check raster layer names, factor levels, missing values, and predictor classes.


Random forest fails on a factor
Make sure prediction data use the same factor levels as the training data.


Leave-one-site-out performs poorly
The model may depend on site-specific patterns or lack representative training sites.


Spatial methane budget is too large
Confirm that flux density was multiplied by inundated area, not total area unless justified.


WAD2M target year is unavailable
Use a mean monthly climatology from available WAD2M years and report that choice.


Git push fails
Check authentication, remote URL, branch name, and whether you need to pull first.

title: Final Project Roadmap
Vector Data: Mapping Gaps in the Global Methane Tower Network
text:
Models are only as good as the places where we collect data. FLUXNET towers provide direct measurements of ecosystem-atmosphere exchange, and FLUXNET-CH4 towers are especially important for understanding methane emissions from natural ecosystems. But the tower network is not evenly distributed across the planet.
In this workshop, we will use vector data to ask:

If FLUXNET CH4 towers are used to understand natural methane emissions, where are we observing the planet, and where are we guessing?

By the end of the workshop, you will be able to:

Convert tabular coordinates into simple feature objects.
Inspect geometries and coordinate reference systems.
Transform vector data into an appropriate projected CRS.
Measure distances among point locations.
Use country and continent polygons from rnaturalearth.
Use spatial predicates and spatial joins to count towers inside regions.
Calculate polygon area and tower density.
Evaluate basic representativeness of the CH4 tower network.
Write spatial outputs for later workshops.




install.packages("sf")
install.packages("rnaturalearth")
install.packages("tidyverse")
install.packages("ggplot2")





library(sf)
library(rnaturalearth)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)

title: Raster Data: Climate Exposure and Tower Similarity
text:
Vector data told us where FLUXNET CH4 towers are located. Raster data lets us ask what environmental conditions surround those towers. In this workshop, we will use gridded climate layers to evaluate the climate exposure represented by methane tower sites and to identify towers that may be useful analogs for thinking about methane fluxes in Yale-Myers Forest wetlands.
We will ask:

What climate conditions do FLUXNET CH4 towers represent, and which towers are most similar to Yale-Myers Forest?

By the end of this workshop, you will be able to:

Read and inspect raster data with terra.
Understand SpatRaster and SpatVector objects.
Crop and mask rasters to an area of interest.
Use raster algebra to create new climate layers.
Summarize raster values globally and by polygon zones.
Extract raster values to point locations.
Compare tower-site climate exposure to gridded climate space.
Calculate environmental dissimilarity among tower sites.
Use multidimensional scaling (MDS) to visualize which towers are more or less similar.




install.packages("sf")
install.packages("terra")
install.packages("tidyverse")
install.packages("rnaturalearth")





library(terra)
library(tidyverse)
library(rnaturalearth)
library(sf)

library(rnaturalearth)
library(sf)

title: Additional Resources: Data Publication
text:
A research project can involve multiple moving parts that could get lost if there isn’t a plan in place to manage how your research will be preserved and reused. This page will get you familiarized with the best practices of data management/publication, and serves as a quick-start guide to the EDI and KNB data repositories.
Some content on this page have been adapted from NCEAS’ coreR for Delta Science Program.

title: Additional Resources: Data Publication
text:
A research project can involve multiple moving parts that could get lost if there isn’t a plan in place to manage how your research will be preserved and reused. This page will get you familiarized with the best practices of data management/publication, and serves as a quick-start guide to the EDI and KNB data repositories.
Some content on this page have been adapted from NCEAS’ coreR for Delta Science Program.

title: Additional Resources: Data Publication
text:
Data Life Cycle



Source: Faundeen et al 2013, USGS
As you begin a research project, your data may go through lots of stages in its life cycle. Like the diagram from Faundeen et al 2013 shows, the first stage starts with planning out goals you want to accomplish or research questions you want to answer. Consider the resources (data, funding, personnel) you will need.
With a plan in mind, the second stage of the data life cycle involves identifying and evaluating new or existing data sources to be collected/generated/re-used. You might acquire datasets at one of the many data repositories out there. At the next stage, these data inputs will be processed through data cleaning and wrangling.
Once the data is processed, it will be ready for analysis, where new insights are generated. Be sure to document the processes that were involved, for example, recording the methods and organizing files to ensure that your workflow is reproducible.
As the research project nears its end, consider how you will preserve your data products for the long-term, especially if it’s required by your funding. If you are planning to submit your data/code for archival at a data repository, plan what metadata and documentation you will need to ensure your results will be accessible and reusable for the future. Each repository will have slightly different requirements, but check out the next few sections to learn more about what data publication at EDI and KNB entails.
Last but not least, once your data is published with a DOI (digital object identifier), other researchers will be able to use and cite your work in the future, which in turn, will expose your research to an even wider audience.
You may have also noticed three arrows cutting across the diagram. These steps should always be kept in mind when you’re progressing your research in order to manage your data effectively.
As you’re working, describe your data products by documenting its metadata. Metadata, or data about data, is a critical component of data reusability. More on metadata below. Additionally, have some quality control techniques in place so that the values of your output are expected. This helps to ensure that your workflow will run as intended. Finally, it never hurts to backup your data regularly to protect it from unexpected data losses due to software failures or natural disasters.

title: Additional Resources: Data Publication
text:
Metadata
As mentioned, metadata plays an important role in the data life cycle, yet it’s often overlooked. Metadata is crucial to understanding, interpreting, and reusing datasets. It contains information on what was measured, who made the measurements, when and where were measurements made, and why was the data collected. The point is to have enough metadata so that anyone (even you) can reuse the data far into the future.
If you are submitting your dataset/code to a data repository, you will need to create metadata that documents all the details about the data itself. One popular, standard format for metadata files in the field of environmental sciences is EML, Ecological Metadata Language. This format arranges the metadata into an organized, machine-readable set of elements.
There are many ways to create this EML file, such as programmatically through R or by filling out a web form offered by a data repository. EDI and KNB are two data repositories that have the web form option for researchers to generate their metadata file.

title: Additional Resources: Data Publication
section: What to preserve from a modeling project
text:
What to preserve from a modeling project
A methane flux modeling project produces more than one final figure. Preserve the pieces that would let someone understand, check, and reuse your workflow.








Product
Why it matters
Example




raw data source notes
documents where data came from and how it was accessed
data inventory table, download script, DOI list


processed modeling table
records the exact rows and predictors used to fit the model
methane_model_data.RDATA or methane_model_data.csv


model-fitting code
allows the model to be refit or audited
.qmd or .R script


model object
stores the trained model used for prediction
random_forest_model.RDATA


validation results
documents model performance and limits
train/test metrics, leave-one-site-out table


sensitivity results
documents model response and extrapolation risk
scenario table, sensitivity curves


spatial prediction rasters
stores mapped flux density or emissions
GeoTIFF files


final report
explains the scientific question, methods, results, and interpretation
PDF, HTML, or Word export


README
tells another person how to navigate the project
plain-text or Markdown file




Metadata for model products
For model-ready tables and rasters, metadata should include:

variable names and plain-language descriptions;
units, including any unit conversions;
spatial resolution, CRS, and extent for rasters;
temporal resolution and time period;
missing-value codes;
data sources and citations;
model formula or predictor list;
known limitations, such as extrapolation or representativeness concerns.



README template
# Project title

## Question
Briefly state the methane flux question and study region.

## Files
- data/raw/: original downloaded data or source notes
- data/products/: processed tables, rasters, and model objects
- scripts/ or qmd/: reproducible workflow
- report/: final report and figures

## Data sources
List each dataset, citation, URL or DOI, access date, units, and processing notes.

## Workflow
1. Compile data
2. Fit model
3. Validate model
4. Run sensitivity analysis
5. Make spatial predictions
6. Scale by inundation area

## Reuse notes
Describe package requirements, API keys that are needed, files that are too large to include, and any known limitations.

title: Additional Resources: Data Publication
text:
EDI Quick-Start Guide
EDI, or the Environmental Data Initiative, is a DataONE data repository that hosts ecological and environmental data for the long-term. They have staff to support curation efforts and work closely with the LTER Network to promote best practices for FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable) data.
To get started with your data package submission, navigate to the Publish Data page in EDI. Click to login to their ezEML metadata editor. ezEML is EDI’s easy-to-use web-based tool for creating metadata.



Choose one of the many options to login to ezEML. You will want to stay consistent with the account that you’re using to login because your submissions will only be associated to the account you used to login.



Once you’ve logged in, click on EML Documents then New.. to start a new EML document.



You will be prompted to enter a name for your metadata document. Enter a unique, memorable name.



Then you will be presented with a form to fill out all the information about your data, such as the Title, Data Tables, Creators, Contacts, etc. Be as descriptive as you can. Don’t worry about filling it all out in one sitting. The information you enter will be saved as you go, and you can always return to your metadata document to continue where you’ve left off.



If you’re not sure how to fill out a section, click on the question mark to get helpful tips.



In the screenshot below, a pop-up appeared on how to best come up with a Title for your data package.



If you have tabular data in CSV files, you can upload them in Data Tables and EDI will automatically infer many attributes about your data, like column names, so you can save some time on manually filling out information. Be sure to double-check the metadata so everything looks correct.



For all other types of data, you can upload them in Other Entities and fill out the metadata as appropriate.



For extra help, check out the ezEML User Guide or email EDI for support. You can also watch their YouTube tutorial below.


Once you’re done filling out the sections to the best of your ability, click on Submit/Share Package to submit your data package for the EDI team to review. When you’re submitting for review, there’s an option to add a message to your submission. If you wish for your data to be embargoed, you should mention it here.
The EDI team will respond back to you via email with any additional suggestions/edits or the greenlight to publish your data package with a DOI if everything looks good.



There’s also an option on this page to invite your colleagues to collaborate on editing the metadata. Be aware that only one person can edit the metadata at a time.
When your data is published with a DOI, the landing page for your data package may look something like this:



The full metadata will be available under View Full Metadata. With this, your data will be available for reuse!

title: Additional Resources: Data Publication
text:
KNB Quick-Start Guide
Just like EDI, KNB, or the Knowledge Network for Biocomplexity, is another DataONE data repository that preserves ecological and environmental data for the long-term. They are hosted by NCEAS and abide by the FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable) data principles.
Navigate to the Submit page to get started on your submission. It will prompt you to sign-in through your ORCID account if you’re not already signed-in. Be sure to create an ORCID account if you don’t have one already. It will help you keep track of the products of your research.






Once you’ve signed-in, the Submit page will direct you to fill out a web form. The top half of the form is for uploading and describing your data files while the bottom half is for the other metadata fields like Overview, People, Dates, Locations, etc.



Click on + Add Files to add any data files you wish to submit. Once you have them uploaded, there are options to either Describe, Replace, or Remove the file.



Click on Describe to enter metadata about the data files. Provide a file description and information about the attributes/variables in this data file.






Once you’re done entering the metadata for the data files, you will also need to fill out the rest of the metadata fields in the bottom half of the web form. Fill out the Overview, People, Dates, Locations, Taxa, and Methods sections as best as you can.



Like with EDI, you do not need to worry about submitting all your metadata in one sitting. Utilize the Save dataset button to save your progress and feel free to continue another day.



After you’ve saved your dataset once, you can come back to the same submission to edit it however you like. Unlike EDI, you can publish your data package in KNB by assigning it a DOI yourself. Click on Publish with DOI whenever you’re ready. Of course, you can continue to edit your data even after assigning it a DOI, but be aware that a new DOI will be issued for every version of your submission.



For extra assistance, email KNB (knb-help@nceas.ucsb.edu) or browse other data packages on KNB to get an idea of what the final product should look like.

title: Additional Resources: Data Publication
section: Accessing Data from Repositories with R
text:
Accessing Data from Repositories with R
Now that you have your data published, you can access it via R. Below is an example script on how to get data from EDI and KNB.

## --------------------------------------------- ##
#      How to pull data from EDI or KNB
## --------------------------------------------- ##
# Script author(s): Angel Chen

# Purpose:
## This script shows you how to either read in data directly from EDI/KNB, 
## or download the data first and then read in

## --------------------------------------------- ##
#               Housekeeping -----
## --------------------------------------------- ##

# Load necessary packages
library(readr) 
library(EDIutils)
library(curl)

## --------------------------------------------- ##
#               EDI example -----
## --------------------------------------------- ##

# Getting a single file ------------------------------------------

# As an example, let's get ER_contour.csv from this EDI package:
# https://doi.org/10.6073/pasta/9b0cce476cb9a7f96575ff1c3d3155c3

# First specify the direct URL to the data file you want
# You can copy any data file's URL by clicking on "View Full Metadata"
# Scroll down to the "Detailed Metadata" -> "Data Entities" section
# Copy the URL associated with the data file you want

# Paste the direct URL to ER_contour.csv
edi_url <- "https://pasta.lternet.edu/package/data/eml/edi/1790/1/e64ec185b099968d9ae653d620e23d31"

# Reading directly 
df <- readr::read_csv(edi_url)

# Or you can download first, and then read in
curl::curl_download(url = edi_url, 
                    destfile = "ER_contour.csv", 
                    quiet = F)

df <- readr::read_csv("ER_contour.csv")

# Getting multiple files -----------------------------------------

# If you would like to download the entire data package as a zip file, 
# use EDIutils::read_data_package_archive()

# Create an empty folder to store the zip file in
dir.create(path = "edi_data")

# Download the data package by specifying the package ID number
# The package ID is listed on the landing page of every data package
EDIutils::read_data_package_archive("edi.1790.1", path = "edi_data")

# Unzip the contents into a new "edi.1790.1" folder
unzip(zipfile = file.path("edi_data", "edi.1790.1.zip"), 
      exdir = file.path("edi_data", "edi.1790.1"))

## --------------------------------------------- ##
#                KNB example -----
## --------------------------------------------- ##

# Getting a single file ------------------------------------------

# This method works for KNB repositories as well!
# Let's get Everglades_Aquatic_Metabolism_SRS_2.csv from this KNB package:
# https://doi.org/10.5063/JQ0ZDM

# First specify the direct URL to the data file you want
# You can copy any data file's URL by right-clicking its Download button (appears as a cloud icon),
# and then selecting "Copy Link Address"

# Paste the direct URL to Everglades_Aquatic_Metabolism_SRS_2.csv
knb_url <- "https://knb.ecoinformatics.org/knb/d1/mn/v2/object/urn%3Auuid%3A9e3cdf01-47b9-4025-8656-75dcf696a46c"

# Reading directly
df <- readr::read_csv(knb_url)

# Or you can download first, and then read in
curl::curl_download(url = knb_url, 
                    destfile = "Everglades_Aquatic_Metabolism_SRS_2.csv", 
                    quiet = F)

df <- readr::read_csv("Everglades_Aquatic_Metabolism_SRS_2.csv")

# Getting multiple files -----------------------------------------

# If you would like to download the entire data package as a zip file,
# right-click the data package's Download button (appears as a cloud icon),
# and then select "Copy Link Address"

# Paste the URL to the entire data package
knb_pkg_url <- "https://knb.ecoinformatics.org/knb/d1/mn/v2/packages/application%2Fbagit-1.0/urn%3Auuid%3Ab9539caf-9133-4d93-8099-3a38b1c4d568"

# Create an empty folder to store the zip file in
dir.create(path = "knb_data")

# Download the data package as a zip file
curl::curl_download(url = knb_pkg_url, 
                    destfile = file.path("knb_data", "example_knb_pkg.zip"), 
                    quiet = F)

# Unzip the contents into a new "example_knb_pkg" folder
unzip(zipfile = file.path("knb_data", "example_knb_pkg.zip"), 
      exdir = file.path("knb_data", "example_knb_pkg"))

title: Sensitivity Analysis: Stress Testing a Methane Flux Model
text:
In the Random Forest workshop, we built a model to predict monthly methane flux. A prediction is only useful if we understand how the model responds to changing inputs and where the model is being asked to extrapolate beyond the data it learned from.
In this workshop, we will ask:

What does our methane model believe about climate, vegetation, and ecosystem controls, and can we trust those beliefs for Yale-Myers-like wetland conditions?

By the end of this workshop, you will be able to:

Identify which predictors are used by a fitted random forest model.
Summarize the training conditions used to fit the model.
Build one-at-a-time sensitivity curves.
Compare realistic methane flux scenarios.
Flag scenarios that fall outside the model’s training range.
Build two-way sensitivity heatmaps to explore interactions.
Compare sensitivity results with random forest variable importance.
Write a short model-risk interpretation for final project reporting.




install.packages("randomForest")
install.packages("tidyverse")
install.packages("ggplot2")





library(randomForest)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)

title: Sensitivity Analysis: Stress Testing a Methane Flux Model
text:

In this workshop, we will ask:

By the end of this workshop, you will be able to:

Write a short model-risk interpretation for final project reporting.




install.packages("randomForest")
install.packages("tidyverse")
install.packages("ggplot2")





library(randomForest)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)

title: Data Integration: Building a Methane Flux Modeling Table
text:
The final course project asks you to build a methane flux model. Before you can fit a model, you need to compile a dataset where each row is a clean observation and each column is a response variable, predictor, or useful descriptor.
In this workshop, we will ask:

How do we combine flux measurements, site metadata, vegetation indices, soil, climate, elevation, and reanalysis data into one table that can be used to model methane flux?

The target data product is a site-month modeling table:

one row = one tower site in one month;
response = methane flux (FCH4_F_gC);
dynamic predictors = monthly tower climate, MODIS vegetation indices, ERA5 climate;
static predictors = ecosystem type, country, soil, climate normals, elevation.

By the end of this workshop, you will be able to:

Identify whether a data source is tabular, vector, or raster.
Identify whether a predictor is static or dynamic.
Choose join keys for site-only and site-time datasets.
Extract raster values to tower locations.
Join static site attributes to monthly observations.
Join dynamic monthly predictors by SITE_ID and YearMon.
Check joins for missing values, duplicate keys, and row-count changes.
Save a model-ready methane flux dataset.

title: Data Integration: Building a Methane Flux Modeling Table
text:
The final course project asks you to build a methane flux model. Before you can fit a model, you need to compile a dataset where each row is a clean observation and each column is a response variable, predictor, or useful descriptor.
In this workshop, we will ask:

How do we combine flux measurements, site metadata, vegetation indices, soil, climate, elevation, and reanalysis data into one table that can be used to model methane flux?

The target data product is a site-month modeling table:

one row = one tower site in one month;
response = methane flux (FCH4_F_gC);
dynamic predictors = monthly tower climate, MODIS vegetation indices, ERA5 climate;
static predictors = ecosystem type, country, soil, climate normals, elevation.

By the end of this workshop, you will be able to:

Identify whether a data source is tabular, vector, or raster.
Identify whether a predictor is static or dynamic.
Choose join keys for site-only and site-time datasets.
Extract raster values to tower locations.
Join static site attributes to monthly observations.
Join dynamic monthly predictors by SITE_ID and YearMon.
Check joins for missing values, duplicate keys, and row-count changes.
Save a model-ready methane flux dataset.

title: Data Integration: Building a Methane Flux Modeling Table
text:
Assignment
Submit a 1-2 page Final Project Data Integration Plan. Include:

A short description of the methane flux question you want to answer.
A table of at least five candidate predictors with source, units, spatial resolution, temporal resolution, static/dynamic status, and join key.
One paragraph describing likely missingness, scale mismatch, or join problems.
A short workflow diagram or pseudocode showing how you will compile your modeling table.
One validation check you will use before modeling.
A brief plan for comparing flux mean and variability across sites, including which predictors you would use to calculate tower dissimilarity.

title: Data Basics
text:
Yale-Myers Forest is a 7,840 acre research forest in northeastern Connecticut. It is a hub for education, research, and harvesting operations within the Yale Forest School system. The forest primarily consists of mixed hardwoods on glacial till soils, with hemlock, scattered white pine stands from old field origins, and red pine plantations established in the 1940s.
In this workshop, we will use daily climate data to ask a more interesting question:

Has Yale-Myers Forest become warmer, wetter, or more climatically stressful since 1990?

By the end of the workshop, you will have built a small climate evidence brief for Yale-Myers Forest. Along the way, you will learn to:

Download climate data with daymetr.
Manipulate data with tidyverse:

Format date elements.
Use select() to choose variables from a data frame.
Use filter() to choose data based on values.
Use mutate() to create new variables.
Use reframe() to summarize datasets.
Use full_join() to merge datasets.

Visualize data with ggplot2.
Write basic functions that make your analysis easier to reuse.

title: Data Basics
text:
Yale-Myers Forest is a 7,840 acre research forest in northeastern Connecticut. It is a hub for education, research, and harvesting operations within the Yale Forest School system. The forest primarily consists of mixed hardwoods on glacial till soils, with hemlock, scattered white pine stands from old field origins, and red pine plantations established in the 1940s.
In this workshop, we will use daily climate data to ask a more interesting question:

Has Yale-Myers Forest become warmer, wetter, or more climatically stressful since 1990?

By the end of the workshop, you will have built a small climate evidence brief for Yale-Myers Forest. Along the way, you will learn to:

Download climate data with daymetr.
Manipulate data with tidyverse:

Format date elements.
Use select() to choose variables from a data frame.
Use filter() to choose data based on values.
Use mutate() to create new variables.
Use reframe() to summarize datasets.
Use full_join() to merge datasets.

Visualize data with ggplot2.
Write basic functions that make your analysis easier to reuse.

title: Spatial Model Visualization: Scaling Methane Flux in Space
text:
In the Random Forest and Sensitivity Analysis workshops, we built and stress-tested a methane flux model. In this final workshop, we will project a model across Connecticut and turn gridded flux predictions into a methane emissions budget.
The key idea is a unit conversion:

methane flux density x inundated area = methane emissions

Our random forest predicts methane flux as g C m-2 month-1. That is a rate per square meter. A map of predicted flux is not yet an emissions budget. To estimate emissions, we need to multiply by the area where methane-producing wetlands are present.
That is where the Wetland Area and Dynamics for Methane Modeling (WAD2M) product enters. WAD2M provides monthly wetland/inundation fractions on a global 0.25 degree grid. Zhang et al. (2021) describe WAD2M version 1.0 as a monthly product for 2000-2018 built for large-scale methane modeling. The NASA UpCH4 methane emissions product uses WAD2M to weight gridded methane flux predictions by fractional wetland extent before converting them to by-grid-cell emissions.
Useful references:

WAD2M paper: Zhang et al. 2021, Earth System Science Data
WAD2M metadata summary: EPA HERO record
Example methane upscaling product using WAD2M: NASA Open Data Portal, UpCH4







Data dependency



This workshop reuses the ERA5 climate rasters saved in data_integration.qmd: data/products/era5_ppt_monthly.tif and data/products/era5_tmean_monthly.tif.
For WAD2M, this workshop expects a Connecticut-ready 2018 monthly inundation fraction raster at data/products/wad2m_inundation_fraction_ct_2018.tif. If that file is not present, the code creates a clearly labeled seasonal classroom proxy from the provided MODIS land-cover raster so the unit-conversion workflow still runs.


By the end of this workshop, you will be able to:

Identify the variables, units, names, and classes needed for spatial prediction.
Align predictor rasters so they have the same CRS, resolution, extent, and layer names.
Predict monthly methane flux density in space.
Explain what WAD2M inundation fraction represents.
Convert flux density to cell-level monthly and annual emissions.
Compare budgets with and without inundation-fraction scaling.
Extract predictions and emissions for the Yale-Myers Forest grid cell.
Test how sensitive the budget is to inundation-area uncertainty.
Report methane emissions in g C, Tg C, and Tg CH4.




library(randomForest)
library(terra)
library(tidyverse)

title: Spatial Model Visualization: Scaling Methane Flux in Space
text:
In the Random Forest and Sensitivity Analysis workshops, we built and stress-tested a methane flux model. In this final workshop, we will project a model across Connecticut and turn gridded flux predictions into a methane emissions budget.
The key idea is a unit conversion:

methane flux density x inundated area = methane emissions

Our random forest predicts methane flux as g C m-2 month-1. That is a rate per square meter. A map of predicted flux is not yet an emissions budget. To estimate emissions, we need to multiply by the area where methane-producing wetlands are present.
That is where the Wetland Area and Dynamics for Methane Modeling (WAD2M) product enters. WAD2M provides monthly wetland/inundation fractions on a global 0.25 degree grid. Zhang et al. (2021) describe WAD2M version 1.0 as a monthly product for 2000-2018 built for large-scale methane modeling. The NASA UpCH4 methane emissions product uses WAD2M to weight gridded methane flux predictions by fractional wetland extent before converting them to by-grid-cell emissions.
Useful references:

WAD2M paper: Zhang et al. 2021, Earth System Science Data
WAD2M metadata summary: EPA HERO record
Example methane upscaling product using WAD2M: NASA Open Data Portal, UpCH4







Data dependency



This workshop reuses the ERA5 climate rasters saved in data_integration.qmd: data/products/era5_ppt_monthly.tif and data/products/era5_tmean_monthly.tif.
For WAD2M, this workshop expects a Connecticut-ready 2018 monthly inundation fraction raster at data/products/wad2m_inundation_fraction_ct_2018.tif. If that file is not present, the code creates a clearly labeled seasonal classroom proxy from the provided MODIS land-cover raster so the unit-conversion workflow still runs.


By the end of this workshop, you will be able to:

Identify the variables, units, names, and classes needed for spatial prediction.
Align predictor rasters so they have the same CRS, resolution, extent, and layer names.
Predict monthly methane flux density in space.
Explain what WAD2M inundation fraction represents.
Convert flux density to cell-level monthly and annual emissions.
Compare budgets with and without inundation-fraction scaling.
Extract predictions and emissions for the Yale-Myers Forest grid cell.
Test how sensitive the budget is to inundation-area uncertainty.
Report methane emissions in g C, Tg C, and Tg CH4.




library(randomForest)
library(terra)
library(tidyverse)

title: Spatial Model Visualization: Scaling Methane Flux in Space
text:
Prepare WAD2M for Connecticut
The exact WAD2M download workflow depends on the format you obtain from the data portal. The key preparation steps are always the same:

Load the global WAD2M inundation-fraction product.
Select the 12 monthly layers for 2018.
Crop the product to Connecticut.
Project and resample it to the same grid as the model predictions.
Save the prepared raster as data/products/wad2m_inundation_fraction_ct_2018.tif.

The following code is a template for preparing a local WAD2M NetCDF or GeoTIFF file after download:

wad2m_global <- rast("path/to/WAD2M_monthly_inundation_fraction.nc")

# Replace this pattern with the actual layer names in your WAD2M file.
wad2m_2018 <- wad2m_global[[grepl("2018", names(wad2m_global))]]

wad2m_ct_2018 <- wad2m_2018 %>%
  crop(ct_extent) %>%
  project(igbp_ct_upland) %>%
  resample(igbp_ct_upland, method = "bilinear") %>%
  clamp(lower = 0, upper = 1, values = TRUE)

names(wad2m_ct_2018) <- sprintf("wad2m_fraction_m%02d", 1:12)

writeRaster(wad2m_ct_2018,
            "data/products/wad2m_inundation_fraction_ct_2018.tif",
            overwrite = TRUE)

title: Spatial Model Visualization: Scaling Methane Flux in Space
section: If your target year is not available
text:
If your target year is not available
WAD2M may not cover the year you want to model. For example, WAD2M version 1.0 covers 2000-2018, but your final project might need a later year. In that case, do not leave inundation fraction out of the budget. Instead, calculate a mean monthly inundation climatology from the WAD2M years that are available.
The idea is:

average all available January layers to create a typical January inundation fraction;
average all available February layers to create a typical February inundation fraction;
repeat for all 12 months;
use those 12 monthly averages to scale the target year’s flux predictions.

This preserves seasonal wetland-area dynamics, even though it cannot represent the exact wetland extent of the missing year.

target_year <- 2021

wad2m_global <- rast("path/to/WAD2M_monthly_inundation_fraction.nc")

wad2m_dates <- tibble(
  layer = seq_len(nlyr(wad2m_global)),
  layer_name = names(wad2m_global)
) %>%
  mutate(
    year = as.integer(str_extract(layer_name, "\\d{4}")),
    month = as.integer(str_extract(layer_name, "(?<=-)\\d{2}|(?<=_)\\d{2}$"))
  )

target_layers <- wad2m_dates %>%
  filter(year == target_year)

if (nrow(target_layers) == 12) {
  wad2m_target <- wad2m_global[[target_layers$layer]]
} else {
  wad2m_monthly_climatology <- map(1:12, function(month_id) {
    month_layers <- wad2m_dates %>%
      filter(month == month_id) %>%
      pull(layer)

    mean(wad2m_global[[month_layers]], na.rm = TRUE)
  })

  wad2m_target <- rast(wad2m_monthly_climatology)
}

wad2m_target_ct <- wad2m_target %>%
  crop(ct_extent) %>%
  project(igbp_ct_upland) %>%
  resample(igbp_ct_upland, method = "bilinear") %>%
  clamp(lower = 0, upper = 1, values = TRUE)

names(wad2m_target_ct) <- sprintf("wad2m_fraction_m%02d", 1:12)

writeRaster(wad2m_target_ct,
            sprintf("data/products/wad2m_inundation_fraction_ct_%s.tif", target_year),
            overwrite = TRUE)







Report this clearly



If you use a monthly climatology because the target year is unavailable, say so in your methods. Write something like: “Because WAD2M was unavailable for the target year, monthly inundation fractions were estimated using the mean WAD2M fraction for each calendar month across available years.”


Load the Connecticut 2018 WAD2M layer if it exists. The file should have 12 layers, one for each month of 2018. If it has one layer, the workflow will reuse that layer for all months. The fallback proxy below is only for teaching the unit conversion; replace it with real WAD2M before using the results scientifically.

wad2m_path <- "data/products/wad2m_inundation_fraction_ct_2018.tif"

if (file.exists(wad2m_path)) {
  wad2m_fraction <- rast(wad2m_path)
  wad2m_source <- "WAD2M inundation fraction"
} else {
  natural_mask <- igbp_ct[[2018 - 2001 + 1]]
  natural_mask[natural_mask %in% c(0, 12, 13, 14)] <- NA
  natural_mask[!is.na(natural_mask)] <- 1

  seasonal_fraction <- c(0.04, 0.05, 0.07, 0.10, 0.12, 0.11,
                         0.09, 0.08, 0.07, 0.06, 0.05, 0.04)

  wad2m_fraction <- rast(lapply(seasonal_fraction, function(x) natural_mask * x))
  wad2m_source <- "seasonal classroom proxy, not WAD2M"
}

wad2m_source

[1] "seasonal classroom proxy, not WAD2M"


Align the inundation fraction layer to the prediction grid:

wad2m_fraction <- terra::project(wad2m_fraction, igbp_ct_upland)
wad2m_fraction <- terra::resample(wad2m_fraction, igbp_ct_upland, method = "bilinear")
wad2m_fraction <- terra::clamp(wad2m_fraction, lower = 0, upper = 1, values = TRUE)

if (nlyr(wad2m_fraction) == 1) {
  wad2m_fraction <- rep(wad2m_fraction, 12)
}

wad2m_fraction <- wad2m_fraction[[1:12]]
names(wad2m_fraction) <- sprintf("wad2m_fraction_m%02d", 1:12)

terra::plot(wad2m_fraction[[1]], main = "January inundation fraction")








Check the fraction values:

global(wad2m_fraction, range, na.rm = TRUE)

                     X1   X2
wad2m_fraction_m01 0.04 0.04
wad2m_fraction_m02 0.05 0.05
wad2m_fraction_m03 0.07 0.07
wad2m_fraction_m04 0.10 0.10
wad2m_fraction_m05 0.12 0.12
wad2m_fraction_m06 0.11 0.11
wad2m_fraction_m07 0.09 0.09
wad2m_fraction_m08 0.08 0.08
wad2m_fraction_m09 0.07 0.07
wad2m_fraction_m10 0.06 0.06
wad2m_fraction_m11 0.05 0.05
wad2m_fraction_m12 0.04 0.04

global(wad2m_fraction, mean, na.rm = TRUE)

                   mean
wad2m_fraction_m01 0.04
wad2m_fraction_m02 0.05
wad2m_fraction_m03 0.07
wad2m_fraction_m04 0.10
wad2m_fraction_m05 0.12
wad2m_fraction_m06 0.11
wad2m_fraction_m07 0.09
wad2m_fraction_m08 0.08
wad2m_fraction_m09 0.07
wad2m_fraction_m10 0.06
wad2m_fraction_m11 0.05
wad2m_fraction_m12 0.04

title: Course Setup
text:
Before starting the workshops, install R, RStudio, and the R packages used across the course. Some packages are used in only one workshop, but installing them now prevents small setup problems from interrupting class time.

install.packages(c(
  "curl",
  "daymetr",
  "dplyr",
  "forcats",
  "GGally",
  "ggplot2",
  "gtools",
  "lubridate",
  "randomForest",
  "readr",
  "sf",
  "terra",
  "tidyr",
  "tidyverse",
  "zoo"
))

These packages are optional or may require additional setup:

# ERA5 downloads require a CDS account and API key.
install.packages("ecmwfr")

# Helpful for spatial examples, but not required by the rendered workshops.
install.packages("rnaturalearth")







Optional data products for final projects



The course data package contains the files needed to run the core workshops. Final projects may require additional products, including ERA5 climate downloads and WAD2M inundation fractions. The Data Integration and Spatial Model Visualization workshops show where those products should be saved under data/products/.

title: Course Setup
text:
Data Acquisition
The data files required for this course are available through the Environmental Data Initiative (EDI) repository. EDI is a data repository that hosts ecological and environmental data for the long-term.
There are various ways to acquire data from data repositories. The method that a lot of people are accustomed to is by clicking “Download” to manually grab the files and then dragging them to the appropriate folder on their local computer. This method works, but it is not the most reproducible way to run your workflow since it’s difficult to document the clicking of your cursor.
Instead, you can acquire data programmatically with R and save all the steps in this part of the workflow in a script. If you, or anyone else, want to reproduce the same results in the future, you can just rerun the same script.
To get the data programmatically through R, first navigate to the data package landing page at: https://doi.org/10.6073/pasta/5100e60582808c66095e767be806109f



The landing page shows a summary of the metadata for this data package. Metadata, or data about data, gives context to the variables in the dataset by documenting what was measured, who made the measurements, when and where were they made, and why was the data collected/created in the first place.
We will need the direct URL to “EDS_Course_Materials.zip” in order to download it through R. To get this specific URL, click on “View Full Metadata”.



This will bring us to a page with detailed descriptions of everything associated with this data package.



Scroll down to “Detailed Metadata” and click on the plus/minus symbols next to “Data Entities” to show more information on “EDS_Course_Materials.zip”.



This section describes what the file is for, along with additional metadata such as the file size and format. Copy the URL for this file.
Once you’ve got the URL, navigate to your workspace/working directory in RStudio for this course. You can check your current working directory by running getwd(). If the output of getwd() does not return the file path to your folder for this course, you will need to run setwd(dir = "...") by replacing ... with the file path of your choice.
Then follow the code below to download and unzip “EDS_Course_Materials.zip”.

## --------------------------------------------- ##
#               EDI Download -----
## --------------------------------------------- ##
# If you don't have the curl package, install it first
# install.packages("curl")

# Load package(s)
library(curl)

# Let's download EDS_Course_Materials.zip from this EDI package:
# https://doi.org/10.6073/pasta/5100e60582808c66095e767be806109f 

# Paste the direct URL to EDS_Course_Materials.zip
edi_url <- "https://pasta.lternet.edu/package/data/eml/edi/2081/1/757761d2f8d14f9026c67a449beac216"

# Download the zip file to your local computer
curl::curl_download(url = edi_url, 
                    destfile = "EDS_Course_Materials.zip", 
                    quiet = F)

# Unzip it into a folder called "data"
unzip("EDS_Course_Materials.zip", exdir = "data")







Having trouble downloading?



If you’re having issues downloading the zip file through R, navigate back to the data package landing page on EDI and click on the Download button under “Data Entities: 1. EDS_Course_Materials”. 


Due to the way the data has been packaged, you may see this folder organization once you’ve unzipped the file:
your-working-directory/
├── data/               
│   ├── __MACOSX/             
│   └── EDS_Course_Materials/             
│       ├── EDS_Course_Materials_README.pdf
│       ├── Elevation.tif
│       ├── Elevation.tif.aux.json
│       ├── ENV720-MOD13A3-061-results.csv
│       ├── final_model.RDATA
│       ├── FLUXNET_FLUXES.RDATA
│       ├── FluxNet_Sites_2024.csv
│       ├── GlobalClimate.tif
│       ├── GlobalClimate.tif.aux.json
│       ├── GlobalSoil_grids.tif
│       ├── GlobalSoil_grids.tif.aux.json
│       ├── MODIS_IGBP_2001-2022_CT.tif
│       ├── MODIS_IGBP_2001-2022_CT.tif.aux.json
│       ├── MODIS_IGBP_2001-2022_CT.tif.aux.xml
│       ├── Monthly_Fluxes.RDATA
│       ├── RANDOMFOREST_DATASET.RDATA
│       ├── SensitivityProducts.RDATA
│       ├── TERRA_PPT_2021_CT.tif
│       └── TERRA_TMEAN_2021_CT.tif   
│
└── more_stuff/            
For the purposes of this course, we need to move all the content under data/EDS_Course_Materials to just the data folder. Run the following code to do so.

## --------------------------------------------- ##
#               Moving Files -----
## --------------------------------------------- ##

# List all the files inside "data/EDS_Course_Materials"
names <- dir(file.path("data", "EDS_Course_Materials")) 

# Create a function to move all the files from "data/EDS_Course_Materials" to "data"
relocate_function <- function(file_name){
  file.rename(from = file.path("data", "EDS_Course_Materials", file_name),
              to = file.path("data", file_name))
}

# Apply the function to all files
lapply(names, relocate_function)

# Delete the now empty EDS_Course_Materials folder
unlink(file.path("data", "EDS_Course_Materials"), recursive = TRUE)

If you’ve followed all the steps correctly, your working directory should now look something like this:
your-working-directory/
├── data/               
│   ├── __MACOSX/             
│   ├── EDS_Course_Materials_README.pdf
│   ├── Elevation.tif
│   ├── Elevation.tif.aux.json
│   ├── ENV720-MOD13A3-061-results.csv
│   ├── final_model.RDATA
│   ├── FLUXNET_FLUXES.RDATA
│   ├── FluxNet_Sites_2024.csv
│   ├── GlobalClimate.tif
│   ├── GlobalClimate.tif.aux.json
│   ├── GlobalSoil_grids.tif
│   ├── GlobalSoil_grids.tif.aux.json
│   ├── MODIS_IGBP_2001-2022_CT.tif
│   ├── MODIS_IGBP_2001-2022_CT.tif.aux.json
│   ├── MODIS_IGBP_2001-2022_CT.tif.aux.xml
│   ├── Monthly_Fluxes.RDATA
│   ├── RANDOMFOREST_DATASET.RDATA
│   ├── SensitivityProducts.RDATA
│   ├── TERRA_PPT_2021_CT.tif
│   └── TERRA_TMEAN_2021_CT.tif   
│
└── more_stuff/
